title: Solving linear systems numerically: backslash, LU, Cholesky, QR & least squares
summary: Master the exact MATLAB commands the course uses to solve $Ax=b$: the backslash `A\b` (Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting), the `lu`, `chol` and `qr` factorizations with forward/back substitution, least squares for overdetermined systems via QR and `\`, the diagnostics `cond` and `norm`, and how to invert with `inv` / via factors — plus why backslash is almost always preferred over forming the inverse. Every snippet is taken verbatim from the Session 2/3 solution files and the FESTA linear-systems slides.
objectives: Solve Ax=b with the backslash operator A\b and explain that it uses Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting
Compute and use the PA=LU factorization with [L,U,P]=lu(A) and solve via two triangular systems (forward then back substitution)
Use chol on a symmetric positive-definite matrix to get A=R'R and solve / invert through the triangular factor
Use qr to factor A=QR and solve square systems via Rx=Q'b
Solve overdetermined systems in the least-squares sense with both the QR method and backslash, and know what \ returns when A is rank-deficient
Quantify accuracy and sensitivity with norm and cond, including the relative error in the infinity norm
Explain why solving with A\b is preferred over computing inv(A)*b on both cost and accuracy grounds

blocks.title: Why this is the most important MATLAB topic

blocks.text: Almost every numerical task in this course ends in a linear system $Ax = b$. The exam expects you to **solve it with the right command** and to **explain why** that command is preferred. This lesson teaches the exact MATLAB tools the sessions use: the backslash `\`, the factorizations `lu`, `chol`, `qr`, the conditioning/norm diagnostics `cond` and `norm`, least squares for overdetermined systems, and how to invert a matrix (and why you usually should not). Every snippet below is taken from the Session 2/3 solution files or the FESTA *Linear systems* slides.

Given a square non-singular matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n,n}$ and a right-hand side $b$, the system $Ax=b$ has a **unique** solution. When $A$ is rectangular with more rows than columns ($m>n$), the system is **overdetermined** and we solve it in the **least-squares** sense (minimize $\|Ay-b\|_2$).

blocks.title: 1. The backslash operator: x = A\b

blocks.text: The single most important command. `x = A\b` solves $Ax=b$ with **Gaussian elimination and partial pivoting**. From the FESTA slides: "`x = A\b` computes the solution x of $Ax=b$ with the Gaussian elimination method and partial pivoting. The command calls specific algorithms depending on the characteristics of the matrix A (diagonal, triangular, symmetric and positive definite, ...); if the matrix has no special structure, the algorithm calculates a generic $PA=LU$ factorization."

blocks.title: Solve a system so the exact solution is all ones
blocks.text:
A standard course trick: build $b$ as the row-sums of $A$, so the exact solution is $x=(1,\ldots,1)^T$. Then `A\b` should return a vector of ones.
```matlab
n = 100;
A = hilb(n);          % some test matrix
b = sum(A,2);         % row sums => exact solution is ones(n,1)
x = A\b;              % solve with Gaussian elimination + partial pivoting
err = norm(ones(n,1)-x,inf)/norm(ones(n,1),inf)
```
`sum(A,2)` sums **along the rows** (dimension 2), giving an $n\times1$ column. `A\b` does the solve. The last line is the **relative error in the infinity norm**.

blocks.title: Backslash is a solver, not an operator on numbers
blocks.text: `A\b` means "$A$ inverse times $b$", solved efficiently. It is **not** the same as `inv(A)*b` numerically — backslash is both faster (about $n^3/3$ vs $n^3$) and more accurate, because it never forms the inverse explicitly. Triangular factors are also solved with backslash: if $L$ is lower triangular, `L\b` runs forward substitution; if $U$ is upper triangular, `U\b` runs back substitution.

blocks.title: 2. LU factorization: [L,U,P] = lu(A)

blocks.text: Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting factors $PA = LU$, where $L$ is **lower triangular with unit diagonal**, $U$ is **upper triangular**, and $P$ is a **permutation matrix** recording the row swaps. Without swaps you get the simpler $A=LU$.

MATLAB gives you both forms:
```matlab
[L,U] = lu(A);      % L is PERMUTED lower-triangular (psychologically lower)
[L,U,P] = lu(A);    % PA = LU, true triangular L, permutation P
```
The two-output `[L,U]=lu(A)` returns a row-permuted $L$ (it equals $P^T\tilde L$). To get a genuinely lower-triangular $L$ and a separate permutation, **always ask for the third output** `P`.

blocks.title: Solve Ax=b via PA=LU (two triangular solves)
blocks.text:
From `session3_solutions_first_part.m`, exercise 3.1, the pivoted solve:
```matlab
[L,U,P] = lu(A);   % computes L and U factors by pivoting (row swaps)
y = L\(P*b);       % forward substitution on Ly = Pb
x_piv = U\y;       % back substitution on Ux = y
```
The system $Ax=b$ becomes $PAx=Pb$, i.e. $L(Ux)=Pb$. Step 1 solves $Ly=Pb$, step 2 solves $Ux=y$. Each triangular solve costs only about $n^2/2$, so once you have the factors, repeated solves are cheap.

blocks.title: Pivoting wins: A=LU vs PA=LU
blocks.text: Exercise 3.1 compares a hand-written `elleu` (no swaps, giving $A=LU$) against MATLAB's `lu` (with pivoting, giving $PA=LU$). The course comment is exact: "the solution obtained by the method of the elimination of Gauss **with pivoting** (i.e. obtained by factoring $PA=LU$) is **more accurate** than without pivoting." Partial pivoting keeps the multipliers small and limits rounding-error amplification.

The hand-written factorization function `elleu` (from the FESTA slide 58 and the session) builds $L$ and $U$ in place, then extracts them with `tril` and `triu`:
```matlab
function [L,U] = ellu(A)
n = size(A,1);
for k = 1:n-1
    for i = k+1:n
        A(i,k) = A(i,k)/A(k,k);          % multiplier m_ik stored below diagonal
        for j = k+1:n
            A(i,j) = A(i,j)-A(i,k)*A(k,j);% update the trailing submatrix
        end
    end
end
L = tril(A,-1)+eye(n);   % strict lower part + identity diagonal
U = triu(A);             % upper triangular part
```
`tril(A,-1)` takes the part **strictly below** the diagonal; `+eye(n)` puts ones on $L$'s diagonal. `triu(A)` takes the upper triangle for $U$.

question.prompt: After `[L,U,P] = lu(A)`, which line correctly solves $Ax=b$?
question.explanation: With $PA=LU$, multiply $Ax=b$ by $P$: $PAx=Pb \Rightarrow L(Ux)=Pb$. First solve $Ly=Pb$ (forward, `y=L\(P*b)`), then $Ux=y$ (back, `x=U\y`). A and D forget the permutation $P$ on $b$ entirely. C applies the factors in the wrong order ($U$ before $L$) and would not give the right answer. Only B matches the session-3 code.
question.theory: PA=LU factorization and two triangular solves.

blocks.title: 3. Cholesky: R = chol(A) for SPD matrices

blocks.text: If $A$ is **symmetric positive definite (SPD)**, there is a unique factorization $A = R^T R$ with $R$ upper triangular and positive diagonal. MATLAB: `R = chol(A)`. Cost is about $n^3/6$, half of LU, because it exploits symmetry. A reliable way to *build* an SPD matrix: take any non-singular $B$ and set $A=B^TB$.

blocks.title: Cholesky solve and inverse (exercise 3.4)
blocks.text:
```matlab
n = 100;
B = diag(10*ones(n,1)) + diag(5*ones(n-1,1),1) + diag(-5*ones(n-1,1),-1);
A = B'*B;            % B nonsingular => A symmetric positive definite
R = chol(A);         % Choleski factor: A = R'*R
b = sum(A,2);
y = R'\b;            % solve R'y = b  (forward, R' is lower triangular)
x = R\y;             % solve Rx = y   (back)
err_sol = norm(ones(n,1)-x,inf)/norm(ones(n,1),inf)
```
`diag(v,k)` puts vector `v` on the $k$-th diagonal: $k=0$ main, $k=1$ super-diagonal, $k=-1$ sub-diagonal. The system $Ax=b$ becomes $R^T(Rx)=b$: solve $R^Ty=b$ then $Rx=y$.

To invert an SPD matrix via Cholesky, invert only the triangular factor:
```matlab
R1 = inv(R);
inversa_c = R1*R1';   % A^{-1} = R^{-1} (R^{-1})^T
```
This exploits $A^{-1}=(R^TR)^{-1}=R^{-1}(R^{-1})^T$, so you invert a single triangular matrix instead of the full $A$.

blocks.title: 4. QR factorization: [Q,R] = qr(A)

blocks.text: Any $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m,n}$ factors as $A=QR$ with $Q$ orthogonal ($Q^TQ=I$, so $Q^{-1}=Q^T$) and $R$ upper triangular (entries $r_{ij}=0$ for $i>j$). MATLAB: `[Q,R] = qr(A)` returns $Q$ of size $m\times m$ and $R$ of size $m\times n$.

blocks.title: Square solve via QR (exercise 3.5)
blocks.text:
For a square non-singular $A$, $Ax=b \Rightarrow QRx=b \Rightarrow Rx=Q^Tb$ (using $Q^{-1}=Q^T$):
```matlab
[Q,R] = qr(A);
x = R\(Q'*b);     % R x = Q' b, one back substitution
```
QR is **more expensive** than $PA=LU$ for square systems. Exercise 3.5 measures the time ratio `t2/t1` of QR over LU with `tic`/`toc` and confirms QR is slower, so for ordinary square systems prefer LU / backslash. QR shines for **least squares**.

blocks.title: 5. Least squares for overdetermined systems

blocks.text: When $A$ is $m\times n$ with $m>n$ (more equations than unknowns), there is generally no exact solution. We minimize the residual $\|Ay-b\|_2$. Two routes: the **QR method** (stable) and the **normal equations** $A^TAx=A^Tb$ (cheaper but can be ill-conditioned). And backslash does it automatically.

blocks.title: Least squares via QR and via backslash (exercise 3.6)
blocks.text:
```matlab
A = [1 2 3 4;-1 0 4 1;3 5 1 0;2 -1 0 1;1 1 -1 1;2 -1 0 3];
b = [1:6]';
[m n] = size(A);
r = rank(A)            % check rank is maximal (= n)
[Q,R] = qr(A);         % QR factorization of the m-by-n matrix
c = Q'*b;              % c = Q' b
Rt = R(1:n,1:n);       % R-tilde: top n-by-n non-singular block of R
c1 = c(1:n);           % first n components of c
cstar = Rt\c1          % least-squares solution: solve R-tilde * x = c1
x = A\b                % SAME result, computed directly by backslash
err = norm(cstar-x)/norm(x)
```
The residual lives in the **last $m-n$ components** $c_2$ that we drop: $\|c_2\|_2$ is the size of the residual. `A\b` performs this whole QR-based procedure internally.

blocks.title: What backslash returns when A is not full rank
blocks.text: FESTA, verbatim: "For overdetermined linear systems $Ax=b$, where $A$ is $m\times n$ with $m>n$ and of maximum rank, `x = A\b` provides the least square solution. **If $A$ is not of maximum rank, the command `\` provides as a least square solution the one with more null components** — this may coincide with the minimum-norm solution but need not." For the guaranteed **minimum-Euclidean-norm** solution when rank is deficient, the course uses `pinv` (the pseudoinverse, built on the SVD).

blocks.title: Polynomial least-squares fit (exercise 3.7)
blocks.text:
Fit a parabola $y=c_1x^2+c_2x+c_3$ to data by building the design matrix and solving with backslash:
```matlab
x = linspace(0,pi,15);          % 15 equispaced x_i in [0,pi]
y = x.*cos(x);                  % the data y_i = x_i cos(x_i)
A = [x.^2' x' ones(15,1)];      % columns: x^2, x, 1
c = A\y';                       % least-squares coefficients c1,c2,c3
xx = linspace(0,pi,100);
p = c(1)*xx.^2 + c(2)*xx + c(3);% evaluate fitted parabola
plot(x,y,'*'); hold on; plot(xx,p,'-r')
```
`x.^2` is **element-wise** squaring (the dot matters); `x.*cos(x)` is element-wise product. `ones(15,1)` is the constant column.

blocks.title: 6. Conditioning and norms: cond, norm

blocks.text: $K(A)=\|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\| \ge 1$ measures sensitivity of $Ax=b$ to perturbations. If $K(A)\approx 1$ the matrix is **well-conditioned**; if $K(A)\gg 1$ it is **ill-conditioned** and small data errors can blow up the solution. MATLAB: `cond(A)` (2-norm), `cond(A,1)`, `cond(A,inf)`.

Norm commands from the FESTA slides:
```matlab
norm(x,1)     % 1-norm of vector x: sum of |x_i|
norm(x,2)     % 2-norm (Euclidean); same as norm(x)
norm(x,inf)   % infinity norm: max |x_i|
norm(A,1)     % matrix 1-norm: max column abs-sum
norm(A,inf)   % matrix inf-norm: max row abs-sum
```
The **relative error** of a computed solution `x` against the exact ones-vector is the recurring exam line:
```matlab
err = norm(ones(n,1)-x,inf)/norm(ones(n,1),inf)
```

blocks.title: The Hilbert matrix is the classic ill-conditioned example
blocks.text: `hilb(n)` builds the Hilbert matrix. The additional exercise solves $Hx=b$ for $n=5,10,15$ with `A\b` and prints `cond(A,inf)` and the error. As $n$ grows, $K_\infty(H_n)$ explodes (e.g. about $3.5\times10^{13}$ at $n=10$) and the solution loses accuracy accordingly — even though backslash is doing nothing wrong. Ill-conditioning is a property of $A$, not of the algorithm.

blocks.title: 7. Inverting a matrix: inv, myinvers, and why backslash wins

blocks.text:
You CAN compute the inverse explicitly. Exercise 3.2 inverts via the LU factors and compares with `inv`:
```matlab
[L,U,P] = lu(A);
inverse_c = inv(U)*inv(L)*P;   % A^{-1} = U^{-1} L^{-1} P
inverse   = inv(A);            % MATLAB built-in inverse
err = norm(inverse-inverse_c,inf)/norm(inverse,inf)
```
This uses $A^{-1}=U^{-1}L^{-1}P$ (from $PA=LU$). The two agree to machine precision.

blocks.title: Never solve Ax=b with x = inv(A)*b
blocks.text: FESTA is explicit: computing the inverse costs about $n^3$ — **three times** the $n^3/3$ of Gaussian elimination — so "solving $Ax=b$ by computing $x=A^{-1}b$ is not cheap." It is also less accurate. Rule for the exam: to **solve a system** use `A\b`; only compute `inv(A)` when the inverse matrix *itself* is the requested object.

question.prompt: You must solve $Ax=b$ for a single square non-singular $A$. Which is the preferred command?
question.explanation: `A\b` solves the system directly with Gaussian elimination + partial pivoting at cost ~$n^3/3$, and is the most accurate. A and D form the explicit inverse (cost ~$n^3$, less accurate) — the course explicitly warns against this. C, the forward slash `b/A`, solves $xA=b$ (right division), which is the wrong system shape here. Backslash is the answer.
question.theory: Backslash vs explicit inverse; computational cost.

blocks.title: 8. Solving many systems sharing the same A

blocks.text:
When you must solve $Ax_i=b_i$ for many right-hand sides with the **same** $A$, factor once and reuse. Exercise 3.3 factors $PA=LU$ a single time, then loops with cheap triangular solves:
```matlab
[L,U,P] = lu(A);     % factor ONCE (cost n^3/3)
tn = P*b;
for i = 1:p
    y = L\tn;         % each step: two triangular solves, cost ~n^2
    x(:,i) = U\y;
    tn = P*x(:,i);    % next right-hand side
end
```
Total cost is about $n^3/3 + p\,n^2$ instead of $p\,n^3/3$ for $p$ separate backslash solves. `tic`/`toc` confirm the factor-once approach is faster for large $n$.

blocks.title: Summary table of the factorizations
blocks.text: From the session slide: **$A=LU$** `[L,U]=lu(A)`; **$PA=LU$** `[L,U,P]=lu(A)` (solve $Ly=Pb$, $Ux=y$); **$A=R^TR$** `R=chol(A)` for SPD (solve $R^Ty=b$, $Rx=y$); **$A=QR$** `[Q,R]=qr(A)` (solve $Rx=Q^Tb$, or least squares $\tilde Rx=\tilde c$). Backslash `\` picks the right one for you automatically based on the structure of $A$.
